segments.s279-g9: بگشتند is a past-tense, 3rd-person plural verb ('they turned/they returned'); in this context it means 'they turned' (changed direction). بسیار here functions as a quantifier 'many' modifying the implied subject. با دگر is a short poetic phrase meaning 'toward/against one another' (با = 'with/toward', دگر = 'other/another'), so the whole segment says that many turned toward each other.
segments.s279-g10: بپیچید is past tense (3rd-person singular) with the prefix بـ- giving the sense 'turned aside' or 'made a turn.' رهام is the proper name (subject). پرخاشخر is an epithet attached to the name ('the quarrelsome/aggressive one') used appositionally; here it functions like an epithet 'the quarrelsome.'
segments.s279-g11: یکی here means 'someone' or 'a certain one' (indefinite subject). نیزه انداخت is a verb phrase: نیزه (spear, direct object) + انداخت (past tense 'threw/ hurled'). بر ران اوی places the target: بر = 'at/upon', ران = 'thigh', اوی = the possessive 'his' (poetic suffix). The word order (object then verb then prepositional phrase) is typical of narrative Persian.
segments.s279-g12: کز is a relative/causal 'which/from which' introducing the consequence clause. اسب اندر آمد is literal 'came into the horse' but here idiomatically means 'he came down from the horse' (اندر آمد + اسب together indicates movement relative to the horse). به فرمان اوی ('by his command' or 'by that effect of it on him') uses فرمان as a noun meaning 'force/effect/command' and اوی as 'his' — grammatically this clause explains the result of the spear-strike.
segments.s279-g13: جدا شد is a compound meaning 'was separated' (جدا = apart, شد = became/was). ز باره means 'from the host/company/occasion' (ز = from, باره = troop/instance), هم آنگاه = 'also then' or 'at once' (هم = also, آنگاه = then/thereupon). The segment ends with ترک (Turk), which functions as the subject/topic picked up in the next clause.
segments.s279-g14: ز اسب اندر افتاد is a straightforward verbal phrase 'fell from the horse' (ز = from, اسب = horse, اندر = into/in, افتاد = fell/was fallen). ترک سترگ is a noun phrase 'the Turk ... سترگ' where سترگ is an epithet attached to ترک; the phrase as a whole means 'the Turk, (the epithet applied),' and completes the clause: 'the Turk fell from his horse.'
segments.s279-g15: به پشت اندرش is a prepositional phrase marking the target 'to/into his back' (به = to/at, پشت = back, اندر + ش = in + his). نیزه‌ای is an indefinite noun 'a spear' (نیزه + یــِــِــِــــٔ ـــای = 'a spear'); زد دگر is the verb 'struck' (زد) followed by دگر 'another' or 'again' indicating an additional blow — the poetic word order places the verb before the modifier.
segments.s279-g16: سنان (the spear-point) is the grammatical subject. اندر آمد is 'came in/entered' (literally 'came into'), so together 'the point entered.' میان جگر is a locative phrase 'into the middle of the liver' (میان = middle/among, جگر = liver/seat of the organs). The verb آمد follows the subject in normal narrative word order to express the motion/result.
segments.s279-g25: به بالا functions as an adverbial phrase meaning 'upwards' (به + بالا). برآمد شده is a resultative/perfect construction: برآمد (arose) + شده (perfect/result marker = 'has become/has risen'), so the clause says that the heart has risen. شاد دل is a noun phrase 'joyful heart' (شاد qualifying دل). Word order is adverbial → verb phrase → noun phrase.
segments.s279-g26: ز درد و غمان is a prepositional phrase 'from pain and sorrows' giving the source/cause. گشته here is the perfect/resultative 'has become/turned' (shows change of state) and links to the predicate complement آزاددل 'free‑hearted' (a compound adjective/noun); together they say 'having become free‑hearted (i.e. free of pain and sorrow)'.
segments.s279-g27: به here introduces the circumstance/instrument ('by/through') — 'by the victory of the king and the lofty throne'. شاه و تخت بلند are coordinated nouns: 'the king's victory' and 'the lofty throne' (تخت بلند = 'lofty throne').
segments.s279-g28: به کام آمده is the idiom 'به‌کام آمدن' meaning 'to have one's desire fulfilled' (به + کام + آمده). زیر بخت بلند is an adverbial phrase 'under/high fortune' where زیر indicates being 'under the sway/subject to' (زیر بخت = subject to fate) and بلند qualifies بخت as 'lofty/high'.
segments.s279-g29: همی is a durative/adverbial particle meaning 'kept on/continually'. آفرین خواند is a fixed expression 'he cried “bravo!”' with خواند as the past-tense verb 'uttered/voiced'. سالار شاه is a title/vocative 'O commander/lord of kings' placed after the verb as the addressee or subject of praise.
segments.s279-g30: ابر شاه is a poetic honorific attached to شاه (an epithet meaning something like 'great' or 'mighty' in context). کیخسرو is the proper name (the legendary king) following the title. و تاج و گاه are coordinated nouns 'and crown and seat/occasion' (تاج = crown; گاه here is a poetic noun paired with تاج). The whole phrase is the content of the acclamation.
segments.s279-g31: که introduces the subordinate/wish clause here ('that' / introducing the blessing). پیروزگر شاه is a compound title 'victor/conquering king' and پیروز باد ends the clause with باد used as the optative particle 'may it be' (a blessing/wish). Word order puts the title before the predicate 'پیروز'.
segments.s279-g32: همه روزگارانش is the subject 'all his days/ages'. نوروز باد repeats the optative construction (نوروز + باد) meaning 'may they be Nowruz' — i.e. 'may all his days be like Nowruz' (a wish for continual renewal/joy). باد again is the optative 'may'.
segments.s279-g17: فرود آمد is a verbal compound (noun + آمد) meaning 'alighted / dismounted' (فرود + آمد). از باره is a poetic locative/temporal phrase ('on that occasion' / 'at that time') where از expresses source/occasion. کرد آفرین is 'he made/uttered "afarin"' — کرد (do/make) + the noun آفرین used as an exclamation (not the verb 'to create' here).
segments.s279-g18: ز here means 'from / by / with regard to' introducing the source or beneficiary of the praise. دادار is the Creator (God). بر + بخت means 'concerning / upon the fortune (bخت)' and شاه زمین is a genitival apposition 'the king of the land' (شاه = king, زمین = land/earth). Word order is source/agent (ز دادار) + preposition + theme (بر بخت) + apposition (شاه زمین).
segments.s279-g19: به کین is a causal phrase 'out of / because of hatred' (به + کین). The verb کشیدش is perfect/past with the enclitic object -ش ('him'): 'he made him endure / caused him to suffer'; نگون is a predicative adjective 'ill‑fated / cast down' describing the result. The agent subject is omitted but understood from context.
segments.s279-g20: ز کینه again gives the motive 'from hatred / spite' (ز + کینه). بمالید is the past of مالیدن 'to smear/apply' (3rd sing.). بر روی is the preposition 'on/upon' + 'face' (روی) and خون is the smeared substance 'blood'. Word order is motive → verb → prepositional phrase of location → object.
segments.s279-g21: به زین اندر is an older/preclassical order meaning 'into the saddle' (به = to/into, زین = saddle, اندر = in/within). آهخت is a past tense 'fastened/seated securely'. و بستش is 'and he bound him' where بست (past of بستن) takes the enclitic -ش 'him'. چو سنگ is a simile 'like a stone'.
segments.s279-g22: This is a compact nominal phrase describing posture: سر آویخته 'his head hung down' (آویخته = hung/pendant) followed by پایها 'the feet' with the locative phrase زیر تنگ 'under the tight (girth / narrow band)'. The possessor is still the same suppressed pronoun referenced by بستش; word order places the descriptive head first then the locative.
segments.s279-g23: نشست here functions as 'he alighted / dismounted' (past of نشستن used for getting off). از بر زین is an archaic phrasing meaning 'from off the saddle' (از + بر + زین). و اسبش کشان attaches a present participial/verbal adjective کشان 'dragging' to اسبش 'his horse', indicating he came with his horse being dragged.
segments.s279-g24: بیامد 'he came' is the main motion verb; دوان is an adverbial/participial form meaning 'running / swiftly'. تا here means 'until' indicating extent, and به جای نشان is the destination 'to the place of the mark' (جای = place, نشان = sign/marker). The clause describes reaching the appointed place by running/urgency.
segments.s279-g1: به پنجم places the action/location as 'in/at the fifth (rank or line)'; چو here is a temporal/introductory particle ('when/as'); the two proper names رهام and گودرز are simply juxtaposed as the people present; بود is the past copula ('was/were') linking that location to the names. Word order is inverted in the poetic line: prepositional phrase + temporal particle + names + past copula.
segments.s279-g2: که is a relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing a clause describing one or both of the named fighters; با marks accompaniment 'with' (here: ‘with Barman’); او is the third-person pronoun 'him/he' (referring to Barman as object); نبرد is the noun 'battle/fight' used as the object of آزمود; آزمود is a past verb 'tried/tested' (perfective), so together نبرد آزمود = 'tried battle / tried to fight'.
segments.s279-g3: کمان is 'bow(s)'; برگرفتند is a past plural verb 'they took up/raised' (subject implied 'they'); و is the coordinating 'and'; تیر and خدنگ are instruments/arms ('arrow' and 'dagger/short sword') listed after the verb by poetic ellipsis. The verb precedes the objects here, and the coordination groups the weapons they seized.
segments.s279-g4: برآمد is an intransitive past verb 'arose/issued forth' placed before its noun phrase (poetic inversion); خروش is 'roar/cry' and سواران جنگ is a genitive phrase 'horsemen of war' = 'the cavalry/horsemen'. Together: 'the war-cry of the horsemen arose.'
segments.s279-g5: کمانها (the bows) is the plural subject; همه and پاک are adverbial/intensifying words ('all' and 'completely/entirely'); بر هم is a frozen/poetic phrase 'against one another'; شکست here functions as the nominal/verb 'broke'/'were broken' — the line uses terse, poetic word order: subject + intensifiers + 'against one another' + verb.
segments.s279-g6: سوی indicates direction 'toward(s)'; نیزه is 'spear' (the target weapon they turn to); بردند is past plural 'they took/carried' (ellipsis: 'they took up spears'); چون is the simile 'like/as'; باد دست literal 'wind-hand' — a compact poetic image meaning 'with hands/quick as the wind' (i.e. very swiftly).
segments.s279-g7: جنگی here is an adjective/noun 'warlike' or 'fighter'; the numeral 'دو' (two) quantifies it (poetic text omits the separate word in the words list). و هر دو means 'and both (of them)'; دلیر 'brave' and سوار 'rider/horseman' are predicative adjectives — together: 'two fighters, both brave and mounted.'
segments.s279-g8: هشیوار is 'alert/awake'; و is 'and'; دیده here is the past participle 'seen' (i.e. 'having seen'); بسی is an intensifier meaning 'much/many' in classical/poetic usage; کارزار is 'battle/contest'. The phrase 'دیده بسی کارزار' means 'having seen many battles' or 'seasoned in battle.'

sentences.s279-s5: Many turned against one another; Roham, the quarrelsome, turned aside.
sentences.s279-s6: One hurled a spear at his thigh; from that he was unseated from his horse.
sentences.s279-s7: He too was separated from the company; immediately the Turk — the great Turk — fell from his horse.
sentences.s279-s8: Another thrust a spear into his back; the spear's point entered into the middle of his liver.
sentences.s279-s13: His joyful heart rose up; freed from pain and sorrow, he became light‑hearted.
sentences.s279-s14: By the king's victory and the lofty throne his desire was fulfilled; he came under a high fortune.
sentences.s279-s15: The commander kept crying 'Bravo!' — 'Hail the king, the mighty King Kēkhusraw, and crown and seat!'
sentences.s279-s16: May the victorious king be triumphant; may all his days be Nowruz.
sentences.s279-s9: He dismounted, and on that occasion uttered praise — "afarin" — to the Creator for the fortune of the king of the land.
sentences.s279-s10: Out of hatred he dealt Siavash a calamity (made him fall); from spite he smeared blood upon his face.
sentences.s279-s11: He fastened him into the saddle and bound him like a stone; his head hung down and his feet were cramped beneath the girth.
sentences.s279-s12: He dismounted from the saddle, and dragging his horse he came running until he reached the marked place.
sentences.s279-s1: In the fifth rank stood Roham and Goudarz, who tried to fight Barman.
sentences.s279-s2: They lifted their bows and (took up) arrows and daggers; the battle-cry of the horsemen arose.
sentences.s279-s3: All the bows were completely broken against one another; they seized spears with hands like the wind.
sentences.s279-s4: Two fighters — both brave and mounted, alert and seasoned by many battles.
